metadata.title: niwo | Software, IT & Cybersecurity
metadata.description: niwo systems is developing a focused software, IT, and cybersecurity consulting practice built around secure systems and incident-response readiness.

Home.heroParagraph: We help startups, small and medium-sized businesses, and individuals understand digital risks, better secure their systems, and adopt new technologies responsibly.

incidentHelp.title: Have you been affected by a cyberattack or suspect a security incident?
incidentHelp.description: Message us directly and briefly describe what happened. We will get back to you as quickly as possible and provide free initial digital guidance: what matters now, which steps you should avoid, and how you can begin limiting the damage.

whoamiLink.title: Read the whoami page
whoamiLink.text: A short profile of my background, current focus, and how I think about practical security work.

Home.hiringNotice: Open to working student & part-time roles — IT-Security · IT-Support · Software Development
Home.servicesDescription: Practical explanations of cybersecurity, secure development, incident response, and AI-assisted engineering — structured to show what matters, where the risks are, and how these ideas work in practice.

approach.title: An honest, practical approach to security
approach.description: I'm Nikita, a Cybersecurity student at IU International University with a background in electrical engineering and technical project coordination. I started niwo systems to combine hands-on learning with real-world practice. Most of what I know comes from building things, taking them apart in controlled lab environments, and figuring out why they fail. This site is where I document that work in a way developers, learners, and small teams can actually use.

scope.title: Focused scope
scope.text: Notes stay centered on the flows, trust boundaries, and actions that carry real application risk.

testing.title: Realistic testing
testing.text: Testing is grounded in how modern web applications are actually attacked, not just checklist coverage.

reporting.title: Clear reporting
reporting.text: Findings and notes are written to be understandable, defensible, and useful without turning into jargon.

remediation.title: Practical remediation
remediation.text: Each topic is meant to make fixes and tradeoffs easier to understand, not just add another checklist.

phishingLab.title: Learn how to spot email scammers
phishingLab.description: A hands-on inbox simulation. Review eight realistic emails and decide: scam or legitimate? Takes about five minutes — no account needed.

aiDevSecOps.title: AI in DevSecOps
aiDevSecOps.description: How AI can support secure software delivery — from planning and code review to CI/CD, infrastructure, and monitoring — without removing human judgment.

agenticEngineering.title: Agentic Engineering
agenticEngineering.description: How developers work with AI agents, tools, permissions, approval gates, and observable workflows while keeping control over high-impact actions.

incidentResponse.title: Incident Response
incidentResponse.description: A practical introduction to triage, containment, evidence preservation, recovery, communication, and learning after a suspected security incident.

common.sourceNote: A compact selection of primary research, standards, and public technical guidance used to ground this article.

metadata.title: AI in DevSecOps, explained | niwo
metadata.description: A clear introduction to using AI across secure software delivery: where it helps, how the workflow works, and why human review remains essential.

aiDevSecOps.title: AI in DevSecOps
intro: DevSecOps means treating security as part of everyday software delivery, not as a final inspection shortly before release. AI can support that work by sorting findings, suggesting tests, explaining unfamiliar code, and highlighting patterns that a busy team might otherwise miss.

The important word is support. An AI system produces predictions from data and instructions; it does not understand a company’s risk, customers, or legal duties in the way accountable people do. Used well, it shortens feedback loops. Used carelessly, it can create convincing new mistakes at the speed of automation.

coreIdea.title: The core idea
paragraphs: Think of a software delivery pipeline as a sequence of checkpoints: plan the change, write code, build it, test it, deploy it, and watch how it behaves. Security questions belong at every checkpoint. AI adds an assistant that can compare, summarize, classify, and propose — while ordinary security controls still decide what is allowed to move forward.

This is different from asking a chatbot whether code is secure. A useful setup combines several forms of evidence: requirements, threat models, code and dependency scans, test results, build provenance, deployment policy, and production monitoring. AI may help connect those signals, but the underlying evidence must remain inspectable.

howItWorks.title: How it works in practice
howItWorks.intro: There is no single AI-in-DevSecOps product or universal workflow. A careful implementation usually adds bounded assistance to an existing secure development process.

steps.title: Plan
steps.text: The team defines security requirements, likely misuse cases, sensitive data, and the actions that always require human approval. AI can help retrieve similar past decisions, but it should not invent the risk tolerance.

steps.title: Code
steps.text: A coding assistant may explain unfamiliar functions, draft tests, or suggest a safer API. The developer reviews the actual diff, checks assumptions, and remains responsible for what enters the repository.

steps.title: Verify
steps.text: Static analysis, dependency checks, secret scanning, and tests produce findings. AI can group duplicates or add context, but deterministic tools and reproducible tests remain the source of evidence.

steps.title: Build
steps.text: The build system records where code and dependencies came from and applies policy gates. An AI suggestion must not bypass signed artifacts, protected branches, or required reviews.

steps.title: Deploy
steps.text: Infrastructure changes are compared with an approved plan. Low-risk changes may be automated; access-control, production-data, and destructive changes should be narrowly permissioned and explicitly approved.

steps.title: Observe and learn
steps.text: Runtime signals, incidents, and false alarms feed back into tests and rules. AI can summarize a noisy event stream, while responders verify the evidence before changing systems or declaring an incident.

whereUsed.title: Where it is useful
whereUsed.intro: The strongest use cases have clear inputs, reviewable outputs, and a safe way to reject the suggestion.

items.title: Code and test review
items.text: Explaining a risky code path, proposing edge-case tests, or helping a reviewer trace data from an input to a sensitive operation.

items.title: Finding triage
items.text: Grouping related scanner results, adding repository context, and drafting a first explanation so specialists can spend more time on the findings that matter.

items.title: Infrastructure changes
items.text: Comparing configuration or infrastructure-as-code changes with policy, then presenting the difference for approval rather than silently applying it.

items.title: Monitoring support
items.text: Summarizing alerts and connecting them to recent deployments, while keeping raw logs, timelines, and source evidence available to responders.

benefits.title: What it can improve
benefits.intro: AI is most valuable when it helps people get useful security feedback earlier and apply existing knowledge more consistently.
items: Shorter feedback loops between a code change and a security question.
Less time spent sorting repetitive findings and searching for context.
More consistent draft documentation, tests, and review prompts.
Better access to security guidance for developers who are not specialists.

risks.title: Risks and limitations
risks.intro: An AI answer can sound certain even when it is wrong. Faster output is not the same as stronger evidence, and automation can amplify both good and bad decisions.

items.title: Insecure or invented output
items.text: Generated code may contain subtle vulnerabilities, depend on nonexistent behavior, or solve the visible test while missing the real requirement.

items.title: Sensitive-data exposure
items.text: Source code, secrets, logs, and customer data can leak through prompts, tool integrations, retention settings, or an untrusted model supply chain.

items.title: Automation bias
items.text: People may accept a fluent recommendation too quickly or stop investigating after the system labels a finding as harmless.

items.title: Adversarial manipulation
items.text: Malicious code comments, issue text, logs, or dependencies can try to steer an AI-enabled tool. Inputs must be treated as untrusted, and tool permissions must stay narrow.

items.title: Changing behavior
items.text: Models, prompts, data, and connected tools change over time. Teams need repeatable evaluations and monitoring rather than assuming yesterday’s behavior will remain stable.

humanResponsibility.title: Human responsibility
paragraphs: Secure delivery is an organizational responsibility. People decide what risk is acceptable, which evidence is sufficient, who may approve a release, and how customers are protected when something goes wrong.

Human review is especially important when a recommendation changes access, handles sensitive data, suppresses a finding, modifies production, or is difficult to reverse. A named owner must be able to explain the decision without relying on the phrase ‘the AI said so.’
items: Define security requirements and risk tolerance
Approve high-impact or irreversible actions
Validate findings against primary evidence
Own incidents, disclosures, and corrective action

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: AI can assist every delivery stage, but it should sit inside an existing secure development framework.
Deterministic checks, protected workflows, and reproducible evidence remain essential.
The safest automations are bounded, observable, reversible, and easy for a person to reject.
Human accountability does not disappear when the system becomes faster or more autonomous.

sources.title: Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) Version 1.1 — NIST SP 800-218
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Secure Software Development Practices for Generative AI — NIST SP 800-218A
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework: Generative AI Profile — NIST AI 600-1
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Guidelines for Secure AI System Development

metadata.title: Agentic Engineering, explained | niwo
metadata.description: A plain-language guide to AI agents, tool use, permissions, approval gates, memory, observability, and the limits of emerging agentic systems.

agenticEngineering.title: Agentic Engineering
intro: An AI agent is a system that can pursue a goal through several steps: inspect the current situation, choose an action, use a tool, observe the result, and decide what to do next. That is different from a chatbot that produces one answer and stops.

Agentic Engineering is the work of designing that loop so it is useful, understandable, and constrained. The field is still emerging: terminology varies, capabilities change quickly, and there is no universally accepted architecture that makes an agent reliable or safe by default.

coreIdea.title: The core idea
paragraphs: A practical agent combines a model with instructions, tools, working state, and a control loop. Tools might read a repository, query a database, open a ticket, or propose a code change. Memory can retain useful context, while policy determines which information and actions are available.

The model’s apparent reasoning is not a guarantee that it understands the world or has reached a correct conclusion. The engineering challenge is therefore to make every important action pass through ordinary controls: authenticated identities, least privilege, structured inputs, validation, logs, budgets, stop conditions, and human approval where the impact is high.

howItWorks.title: How an agentic workflow works
howItWorks.intro: The exact loop differs between systems, but a controlled workflow usually contains the following stages.

steps.title: Scope the task
steps.text: A person or trusted system states the goal, boundaries, available data, success criteria, and actions that are forbidden or require approval.

steps.title: Form a plan
steps.text: The agent breaks the task into steps. The plan is provisional: new evidence may require revision, and a reviewer should be able to inspect the intended direction.

steps.title: Select a tool
steps.text: The system chooses from an allowlist of tools with structured parameters. It should not gain a general-purpose credential simply because one tool needs narrow access.

steps.title: Check permission
steps.text: Policy evaluates identity, task context, data sensitivity, action impact, and budget. A high-impact action pauses at an approval gate.

steps.title: Act and observe
steps.text: The tool performs the bounded operation and returns a result. The agent records what happened, validates the response, and uses it as new evidence rather than unquestioned truth.

steps.title: Stop or escalate
steps.text: The loop ends when success criteria are met, a limit is reached, uncertainty becomes too high, or a person must decide. A safe agent is designed to stop, not only to continue.

whereUsed.title: Where it is used
whereUsed.intro: Agentic systems are most credible when they operate in a defined environment with measurable outcomes and limited consequences.

items.title: Software engineering
items.text: Inspecting a repository, proposing a small change, running tests in a sandbox, and presenting the diff for human review.

items.title: Operations support
items.text: Collecting diagnostic information, correlating recent changes, and drafting a remediation plan without directly altering production.

items.title: Research workflows
items.text: Searching approved sources, extracting evidence, comparing claims, and producing a traceable draft that a researcher can verify.

items.title: Administrative processes
items.text: Preparing a ticket, routing a request, or assembling a report where each external action is logged and sensitive decisions remain with people.

benefits.title: What it can improve
benefits.intro: Agents can reduce the coordination cost of multi-step work, especially when information is spread across several tools.
items: Carry context across several steps instead of restarting with every tool.
Automate repetitive collection and preparation work around expert decisions.
Apply a documented workflow more consistently when the environment is well bounded.
Create useful action trails when tool calls, inputs, outputs, and approvals are recorded.

risks.title: Risks and limitations
risks.intro: Giving a model tools changes the risk: an incorrect sentence can become an incorrect action. Agentic security guidance is developing quickly, so controls need continual testing rather than one-time certification.

items.title: Goal or instruction hijacking
items.text: Untrusted text in a document, website, issue, or tool result may try to redirect the agent away from the authorized task.

items.title: Excessive agency
items.text: A tool may expose more data or authority than the task requires. A small planning error can then have a large operational impact.

items.title: Cascading errors
items.text: One plausible but wrong observation can shape the next plan, tool choice, and action, making a multi-step failure harder to notice.

items.title: Memory poisoning
items.text: Incorrect or malicious information stored as memory can influence later tasks, users, or decisions long after the original input disappeared.

items.title: Poor observability
items.text: If prompts, tool calls, policy decisions, and state changes are not recorded, operators cannot reconstruct why an action occurred.

items.title: Runaway loops and cost
items.text: An agent can repeat unsuccessful actions, consume resources, or create load. Time, action, and spending budgets need hard limits.

humanResponsibility.title: Human responsibility
paragraphs: People choose the goal, design the available tools, grant identities and permissions, define evaluation criteria, and decide which actions need approval. Those are governance decisions, not details that can be delegated to a prompt.

Human oversight must also be realistic. Asking someone to approve hundreds of opaque actions is not meaningful control. Reviewers need enough context, time, authority, and technical evidence to reject or change the proposed action.
items: Set task boundaries and least-privilege access
Approve consequential or irreversible actions
Review logs, evaluations, and failure patterns
Suspend the system and investigate incidents

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: An agent is a model connected to a controlled loop of observation, planning, tools, and feedback.
Useful autonomy depends on narrow permissions, validation, observable actions, budgets, and stop conditions.
Agentic Engineering remains an emerging discipline; impressive demos do not establish dependable real-world behavior.
Humans remain responsible for goals, access, approvals, accountability, and incident handling.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework 1.0
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework: Generative AI Profile — NIST AI 600-1

metadata.title: Incident Response, explained | niwo
metadata.description: A practical introduction to detecting, triaging, containing, investigating, recovering from, and learning after a cybersecurity incident.

incidentResponse.title: Incident Response
intro: Incident Response is the coordinated work that begins when an organization suspects that systems, accounts, or data may have been compromised. The goal is not merely to remove malware. It is to limit harm, understand what happened, preserve reliable evidence, restore trustworthy operations, and communicate responsibly.

A good response balances speed with care. Disconnecting a machine may stop an attacker, but it can also interrupt a critical service or destroy short-lived evidence. Teams therefore work from tested plans, assign clear authority, record decisions, and update their understanding as new facts arrive.

coreIdea.title: The core idea
paragraphs: Incident response is a cycle that connects preparation, detection, response, and recovery. Preparation determines whether logs exist, backups work, contacts are known, and people can make decisions under pressure. Detection and triage turn signals into an evidence-based view of scope and impact.

Response then contains the immediate risk, preserves and analyzes evidence, removes the cause, and restores systems carefully. Recovery is complete only when important services are trustworthy again, stakeholders have the information they need, and lessons from the event improve future prevention and response.

howItWorks.title: How the response unfolds
howItWorks.intro: Real incidents rarely follow a perfect straight line. Teams may repeat these stages as the scope changes, but the structure helps them avoid improvised and contradictory actions.

steps.title: Prepare
steps.text: Define roles, contacts, escalation thresholds, communication channels, evidence procedures, backups, and safe access before an incident occurs. Practice with realistic exercises.

steps.title: Detect and verify
steps.text: Collect alerts, reports, and system signals. Check whether the event is genuine, record initial facts, and avoid treating an unverified theory as the conclusion.

steps.title: Triage and scope
steps.text: Estimate which identities, devices, applications, data, and business processes may be affected. Prioritize by impact, urgency, and the attacker’s remaining access.

steps.title: Contain
steps.text: Limit further harm through actions such as isolating a host, disabling a credential, blocking a malicious connection, or separating a network segment. Choose actions proportionate to operational risk.

steps.title: Preserve and analyze
steps.text: Collect relevant logs, files, volatile data, and timelines in a way that protects integrity and provenance. Analysis tests competing explanations and refines the scope.

steps.title: Eradicate and recover
steps.text: Remove malicious persistence, close the exploited path, rotate affected secrets, rebuild or restore systems, verify backups, and monitor closely for recurrence.

steps.title: Communicate and learn
steps.text: Provide accurate updates through approved channels, meet legal or contractual duties, document decisions, and turn lessons into better controls, plans, and exercises.

whereUsed.title: Where it is used
whereUsed.intro: Incident response applies whenever digital trust may have been lost — from a single account to a major service disruption.

items.title: Account compromise
items.text: A phishing report or unusual login leads to session revocation, credential reset, mailbox review, and checks for further access.

items.title: Ransomware
items.text: Teams isolate affected systems, protect backups, determine the spread, coordinate business continuity, preserve evidence, and plan a verified restoration.

items.title: Cloud or API exposure
items.text: A leaked token or misconfiguration requires rapid credential control, access-log analysis, scope assessment, and validation that the exposed path is closed.

items.title: Application breach
items.text: Responders contain the vulnerable service, preserve application and infrastructure evidence, identify affected data, remediate the defect, and monitor the repaired system.

benefits.title: What good response improves
benefits.intro: A prepared response does not guarantee that an incident will be small, but it improves the organization’s ability to make defensible decisions under uncertainty.
items: Faster containment and less time for an attacker to cause additional harm.
Clearer coordination across technical, legal, leadership, communications, and external partners.
More reliable evidence for scoping, recovery, reporting, and later investigation.
Safer restoration and concrete lessons that reduce the chance of recurrence.

risks.title: Risks and limitations
risks.intro: Response decisions are made with incomplete information. A playbook provides structure, but it cannot replace judgment about safety, business continuity, evidence, law, and the people affected.

items.title: Destroying evidence
items.text: Rebooting, wiping, or changing a system too quickly can remove volatile data and make the sequence of events harder to reconstruct.

items.title: Scoping too narrowly
items.text: Finding one compromised account or device does not prove the incident is contained. Shared credentials, lateral movement, and persistence may extend the scope.

items.title: Operational harm
items.text: Aggressive isolation can interrupt essential services. Containment choices must balance ongoing attacker access against safety and business impact.

items.title: Unreliable recovery
items.text: Restoring from an unverified backup or reconnecting before the root cause is addressed can return the organization to a compromised state.

items.title: Poor communication
items.text: Late, speculative, or inconsistent messages can harm affected people and undermine legal, contractual, and public responsibilities.

items.title: Human strain
items.text: Long incidents create fatigue and tunnel vision. Rotations, decision logs, handovers, and access to specialist support are operational safeguards.

humanResponsibility.title: Human responsibility
paragraphs: Response needs named decision-makers. An incident commander coordinates priorities and records decisions; technical specialists investigate and contain; system owners explain operational consequences; legal, privacy, communications, and leadership roles address duties beyond the technical system.

Tools can correlate alerts, collect artifacts, and automate approved containment steps. People still decide whether the evidence is sufficient, whether an action is proportionate, who must be notified, when operations are trustworthy again, and what accountability follows.
items: Assign authority, roles, and escalation paths
Protect evidence integrity and affected people
Approve containment, disclosure, and recovery decisions
Document lessons and fund corrective action

takeaways.title: Key takeaways
items: Incident response is a coordinated risk-management process, not only a technical cleanup task.
Preparation, reliable evidence, clear roles, and practiced communication matter before the first alert arrives.
Containment must limit harm without unnecessarily destroying evidence or critical operations.
Recovery includes verification, communication, documentation, and learning — not merely switching systems back on.

sources.title: Incident Response Recommendations and Considerations — NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 3
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Federal Government Cybersecurity Incident and Vulnerability Response Playbooks
Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response — NIST SP 800-86
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

sources.title: Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
sources.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology

metadata.title: Web App Security | niwo
metadata.description: A practical topic page on web application security: flows, inputs, trust boundaries, common weaknesses, and plain-language notes on what matters.

card.title: Web App Security
card.text: A practical look at the flows, inputs, and trust boundaries that carry real risk in web applications, with notes on how issues show up and how to reason about fixes.
card.tag: Good for: developers, learners, and small teams who want a clearer mental model of web application risk.

hero.title: How web apps become exposed
hero.intro: This topic collects practical notes on how modern web applications become exposed: weak flows, missing access checks, unsafe input handling, session mistakes, and trust boundaries that are assumed instead of enforced.
hero.outcomeText: A clear, plain-language map of where web application risk tends to appear and how to think about fixing it.

covers.title: What this topic covers
items: The flows that carry real risk: login, account actions, payments, anything sensitive
Authentication and session handling, and the assumptions behind them
Access control gaps: privilege escalation and broken object-level access
Input handling: injection, unsafe parsing, and weak validation
Configuration and trust-boundary issues that quietly widen your attack surface

steps.title: Understand how the system is meant to work
steps.text: Start with the intended behavior: where sensitive actions happen, what data matters, and where the application assumes trust.

steps.title: Look at where risk actually collects
steps.text: Focus on the flows, inputs, and edge cases that tend to hide real issues, not just what a checklist would flag.

steps.title: Write it so it can be understood
steps.text: Good security notes explain impact and fixes in plain language. No vague risk scores; just what is wrong, why it matters, and what would improve it.

bestFor.title: Useful for
items: Developers building products with accounts, permissions, or sensitive data
Learners trying to understand where web security issues come from
Small teams who want a clearer view of application risk
Anyone who wants an honest second pair of eyes on their security thinking

deliverables.title: What I document
items: Short notes on common web application failure modes
Plain-language explanations without jargon nobody reads
Practical fix patterns and tradeoffs
Examples from labs, reviews, and controlled test setups

cta.title: Have a web security question?
cta.text: If this topic connects to something you are building or learning, you can ask a concrete question and I will take a look when I can.

metadata.title: Pentest Preparation | niwo
metadata.description: Plain-language notes on preparing for a penetration test: understanding the process, defining scope, reading reports, and asking a testing provider better questions.

card.title: Pentest Preparation
card.text: Plain-language notes on what a penetration test is, how scope works, what a useful report should contain, and which questions are worth asking before you choose a testing provider.
card.tag: Note: This is a learning and preparation topic, not a claim to sell full penetration tests.

hero.title: Understand pentests before you pay for one
hero.intro: This topic explains what a pentest usually involves, how to think about scope, what a report can and cannot tell you, and how to have a more informed conversation with a testing provider.
hero.outcomeText: A clearer understanding of the process, better scoping instincts, and a practical list of questions to ask before commissioning a test.

covers.title: What this topic covers
items: What a penetration test actually involves, from start to finish
Help defining and scoping what should be tested
What to expect from a report and how to read one
The right questions to ask a testing provider
How to make sure you get real value from the test

reassurance.title: Controlled, not chaotic
reassurance.text: A penetration test is not the same as an actual breach. Done properly, it should not harm your systems or put your data at risk. There is no real damage to recover from, and no fines to worry about. Anything a pentester finds is used for one purpose only: making your security stronger.

steps.title: Understand the process
steps.text: I walk you through what a pentest involves, so there are no surprises and no jargon you have to decode later.

steps.title: Prepare your scope
steps.text: We figure out what's worth testing and define a scope that fits your product and your budget.

steps.title: Go in informed
steps.text: You head into the test knowing what to expect, what to ask, and how to judge the results.

bestFor.title: Useful for
items: Teams considering their first penetration test
Founders who need to meet a customer or compliance requirement
Developers who want to scope a test properly before paying for it
Anyone unsure what to expect from a testing provider

deliverables.title: What I document
items: Plain-language walkthroughs of the pentest process
Scope examples and questions to clarify early
Notes on reading and acting on reports
A list of useful questions to ask a provider

cta.title: Thinking about a pentest?
cta.text: If you are trying to understand scope, reports, or provider questions, ask a concrete security question and I can point you in a practical direction.

metadata.title: Secure Development | niwo
metadata.description: Practical secure-development notes for developers and learners: web security basics, defensive decisions, risk prioritization, and safer implementation patterns.

card.title: Secure Development
card.text: Practical notes on building web applications with fewer security surprises: how to think about auth, input handling, data exposure, dependencies, and tradeoffs while you build.
card.tag: Good for: learners, solo developers, and small teams building web applications.

hero.title: Build with security in mind, without drowning in checklists
hero.intro: This topic is about practical secure development: the small design and implementation choices that reduce risk before an app needs a formal review or a long report.
hero.outcomeText: A practical way to think through security decisions while building: what matters, what can wait, and where mistakes tend to become real risk.

covers.title: What this topic covers
items: How to spot risky assumptions in your current setup
Authentication, authorization, session, and data-flow decisions
Practical web security and secure-development patterns
What to watch out for as an application grows
How to prioritize effort where it actually reduces risk

steps.title: Start from the thing being built
steps.text: Good security thinking starts with the product, stack, data, and real user actions, not a generic checklist.

steps.title: Trace the risky decisions
steps.text: Follow where trust, identity, input, permissions, and external dependencies enter the system.

steps.title: Turn it into practical next steps
steps.text: The useful output is not fear. It is a clearer list of tradeoffs, fixes, and next things to learn.

bestFor.title: Useful for
items: Early-stage teams figuring out their security basics
Solo developers who want someone to sanity-check their thinking
Small businesses moving things online for the first time
Anyone trying to learn secure development in a practical way

deliverables.title: What I document
items: Practical notes tied to real web-development decisions
Honest, jargon-free explanations of common risks
Fix patterns you can actually apply
A prioritized sense of what to learn or improve first

cta.title: Not sure how to reason about a security decision?
cta.text: Ask a concrete question about what you are building, reading, or testing, and I can help you think it through.

metadata.title: About | niwo
metadata.description: About Nikita, a cybersecurity student in Bonn building Niwo Systems in public: real security work, documented honestly, with carefully scoped help where it fits.

About.title: whoami

paragraphs: Hi, I'm Nikita — a cybersecurity student with a technical background in electrical engineering and project work.
With niwo systems I'm building my own web and security project. Along the way, I want to explain cybersecurity topics in a way that makes them understandable, practical, and easy to follow.
This is where I document what I build, test, break, improve — and what I learn doing it.

focus.label: Area of interest
focus.value: Web application security, secure development, AI-assisted DevSecOps, and security hardening

mode.label: Mode
mode.value: Focused reviews, learning notes, research, and small projects

About.signalText: In a digital world that keeps moving faster, I want to give people more orientation: explaining clearly where the risks lie, what security really means, and how to use systems more consciously.

phishing.title: Phishing Defense Lab
phishing.text: An interactive training tool that teaches you to spot phishing in five minutes — no account, no tracking. Built with Next.js; the detection scenarios use stratified difficulty sampling so easy rounds don't repeat.

owasp.title: OWASP Lab Detection Engine
owasp.text: A local-only lab pairing a deliberately vulnerable Flask app with a Python detection engine: twelve OWASP-oriented scenarios (SQLi, XSS, SSRF, broken access control, insecure deserialization, and more), each with a documented detection rule ID, structured JSONL telemetry, secure/insecure lab modes, and pytest coverage in CI.

website.title: This website
website.text: Niwo Systems is open source. If you want to know how I work, read the code — security headers, i18n, legal compliance and all.

foundations.title: Software Development
foundations.text: Alongside security work, I study at IU International University. My object-oriented programming course project — a CLI habit tracker with a layered architecture, salted PBKDF2 password hashing and full pytest coverage — was graded 100%.

now.heading: Right now
paragraphs: I'm studying cybersecurity at IU while building practical experience in web security, IT support, and secure development.

Right now I'm looking for working student, internship, or part-time roles in IT, security, or technical support — especially where I get to work with real systems, documentation, troubleshooting, and security topics.
I am preparing to take on small, clearly scoped web and security projects when the fit is right.

business.label: If you have a security question for your business

nature.title: 2. Nature of the Website
nature.body: This website presents niwo systems, its consulting focus, and its technology and security work. Consulting engagements are agreed separately in writing.
nature.disclaimerText: Information on this website is not a binding offer. The scope, authorization, deliverables, timing, and fees of any engagement are defined in a separate agreement.

contact.title: 3. Contact
contact.body: For legal notices or formal correspondence, please contact legal@niwosystems.com. The external business address is being established and will be published here once finalized.

liability.title: 4. Liability for Content
liability.body: Content is created with care; however, no warranty is given for completeness or accuracy. Links to external sites are provided for convenience, and responsibility for their content lies with the respective providers.

metadata.title: Vulnerability Disclosure Policy | niwo
metadata.description: How to responsibly report vulnerabilities to the niwo security team.

Vdp.title: Vulnerability Disclosure Policy
Vdp.description: Responsible disclosure guidance for researchers reporting potential security issues.

overview.title: 1. Overview
overview.body: Security is a top priority for us at niwo. We appreciate the work of security researchers and the responsible disclosure of potential vulnerabilities.

scope.title: 2. Scope
scope.body: This policy applies to digital assets owned, operated, or maintained by niwo, including niwosystems.com and associated subdomains.

guidelines.title: 3. Guidelines
items: Do not exploit vulnerabilities beyond what is necessary to demonstrate them.
Do not access or modify user data without consent.
Do not disrupt or degrade our systems.
Provide sufficient detail so we can reproduce and validate the issue.

reporting.title: 4. Reporting
reporting.body: If you discover a vulnerability, please report it responsibly by emailing security@niwosystems.com.
reporting.calloutTitle: Include the following information where possible:
items: Description of the vulnerability
Steps to reproduce
Potential impact
Suggested remediation, if available

acknowledgement.title: 5. Acknowledgement
acknowledgement.body: We may publicly acknowledge valid reports that help us improve our security posture. Thank you for helping keep niwo and its users safe.

metadata.title: Privacy Policy | niwo
metadata.description: Privacy policy for this website covering email contact, AWS hosting and delivery, Proton Mail, retention, and tracking.

intro.title: 1. Introduction
intro.body: This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, and protect personal data when you visit this website or contact us by email. We aim to handle information responsibly and transparently in line with applicable data protection law, including the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

controller.title: 2. Data Controller
controller.controllerValue: Nikita Wokurka, operating under the business designation niwo systems
controller.addressValue: Germany; external business address being finalized
controller.body: If you have questions about this Privacy Policy or your personal data, you can contact us using the details above.

dataWeCollect.title: 3. Data We Collect
dataWeCollect.voluntaryIntro: When you contact us by email, we may process:
voluntaryItems: Your name or signature, if you include it
Your company name, phone number, or other contact details, if you include them
The content of your message and any attachments you send
dataWeCollect.voluntaryOutro: This information is used solely to review and respond to your inquiry.
dataWeCollect.automaticBody: When you visit the website, basic technical data may be processed automatically by hosting infrastructure, such as your IP address, request metadata, browser information, and access logs needed for secure delivery of the site.

purposeLegalBasis.title: 4. Purpose & Legal Basis
purposeLegalBasis.body: We process personal data to operate the website securely, respond to inquiries, and protect our systems against abuse or misuse. Depending on context, the legal basis is either our legitimate interests under Article 6(1)(f) GDPR or steps taken at your request prior to entering into a contract under Article 6(1)(b) GDPR.

storageRetention.title: 5. Data Storage & Retention
storageRetention.body: Unsuccessful business inquiries are normally deleted within six months after the last communication. Correspondence relating to an active client engagement is retained for the duration of the engagement and afterwards only where necessary to establish, exercise, or defend legal claims or comply with legal obligations. Contracts and invoices are archived separately under applicable statutory retention requirements rather than kept indefinitely in the ordinary mailbox. For privacy or security requests, we retain only the limited evidence needed to document and resolve the matter, and delete it when it is no longer reasonably required.

thirdParties.title: 6. Use of Third-Party Providers
thirdParties.hostingBody: Amazon Web Services (AWS) provides Amplify Hosting, CloudFront content delivery, CloudWatch operational logging, and S3 media storage. AWS may process IP addresses, requested URLs, timestamps, user-agent information, request headers, and delivery or security metadata to provide and protect the website. The Amplify deployment and media-storage origin are located in Frankfurt, Germany (eu-central-1), while CloudFront uses a global edge network. Amplify compute logs are retained for 90 days. Amplify Hosting access logs are retained by AWS until the Amplify app is deleted. Separate access logging for the media CloudFront distribution is disabled.
thirdParties.emailBody: Business email is provided through Proton Mail by Proton AG in Switzerland. When you contact info@niwosystems.com, legal@niwosystems.com, or security@niwosystems.com, Proton may process sender and recipient addresses, message metadata, message content, signatures, and attachments to transmit and store the correspondence. Proton states that Proton Mail data is stored in encrypted form on servers in Switzerland, Germany, or Norway and that encrypted offline backups may be retained for up to 30 days.
thirdParties.fontsBody: The website uses locally hosted fonts. No font requests are sent to third-party font providers when loading the site.

sharing.title: 7. Data Sharing
sharing.body: We do not sell personal data. Data is only shared where necessary to operate the website, comply with legal obligations, or protect our rights and systems.

rights.title: 8. Your GDPR Rights
rights.intro: Under the GDPR, you may have the right to:
items: Request access to your personal data
Request correction or deletion of your data
Request restriction of processing
Object to processing in certain circumstances
Lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority
rights.outro: To exercise these rights, contact legal@niwosystems.com.

security.title: 9. Data Security
security.body: We take reasonable technical and organizational measures to protect personal data against unauthorized access, loss, misuse, or disclosure. However, no internet transmission or storage system can be guaranteed to be completely secure.

cookies.title: 10. Cookies & Tracking
cookies.body: This website currently does not set a language-preference cookie and does not use non-essential tracking cookies, analytics beacons, advertising pixels, or session-replay tools. If this changes in the future, this policy and any required consent controls will be updated before the new processing is enabled.

changes.title: 11. Changes to This Policy
changes.body: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time to reflect legal, technical, or operational changes. The latest version will always be published on this page.

contact.title: 12. Contact
contact.body: If you have questions about this Privacy Policy or about how your personal data is handled, contact legal@niwosystems.com.
